reviews.firstImpression: The wine list at Valle reads like a geography lesson through Mexico's emerging wine regions — and we mean that as the highest compliment. This is the most ambitious all-Mexican wine program we've seen in Dallas, spanning Valle de Guadalupe to Chihuahua to Guanajuato. It's a bold move that immediately signals this isn't your typical margarita-and-cerveza spot.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: Valle commits fully to the concept with zero hedging: every bottle comes from Mexico. You'll find 12 by-the-glass options pulling from Baja California's Valle de Guadalupe (the obvious starting point), but also deeper cuts from Coahuila, Querétaro, and Guanajuato. The list showcases producers like Monte Xanic, Vinaltura, and Bodegas Magoni alongside lesser-known operations like Piñamora out of Chihuahua and Valle de Jaral de Berrios in Guanajuato. It's an education in what Mexican viticulture is capable of beyond the usual suspects, with varietals ranging from Tempranillo and Malbec to Gewurztraminer and Montepulciano.
reviews.byTheGlass: With 12 glass pours, Valle gives you plenty of room to explore without committing to a full bottle. The selections span white, red, and blends, letting you bounce between a Sauvignon Blanc from Baja and a Syrah-Merlot-Tempranillo blend from central Mexico. It's a smart way to discover what Mexican wine is all about, though we wish there was more rotation to keep regulars coming back.

bestValue.note: A 10% markup on a Chihuahua-grown Italian varietal that drinks clean and honest — practically retail pricing in a full-service restaurant

hiddenGem.note: Most people sleep on Baja Sauvignon Blanc, but Magoni's coastal fruit brings the crisp minerality you'd expect from Loire without the Euro price tag

skipThis.note: At $120 for a $40 retail bottle, you're paying a 200% markup for name recognition — there are better values on this list

perfectPairing.wine: Malbec-Syrah-Tempranillo-Merlot blend from Valle de Jaral de Berrios
perfectPairing.dish: Any grilled meat or mole dish
perfectPairing.note: This Guanajuato blend has the fruit-forward richness to stand up to char and spice while keeping enough acidity to cut through complex sauces

reviews.bottomLine: Valle takes a risk most Dallas restaurants won't: committing entirely to Mexican wine. The markup stings on some bottles, but the educational value and sheer novelty of the list earn it Wild Card status. Come curious, leave converted.
reviews.subtitle: All-Mexican Wine List in the Heart of Dallas

reviews.firstImpression: The wine list lands on your table like a small phone book, and it's not compensating — this is a legitimate 3,500+ bottle cellar with serious depth. First Growths, DRC, Screaming Eagle sit alongside thoughtful producers across Burgundy, Bordeaux, Napa, and the Rhône. This is the kind of place where someone at the next table is ordering a $2,000 bottle without blinking.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: The Bordeaux section alone could stock most wine bars, with verticals of Pauillac and Saint-Julien that go back decades. Napa gets equal treatment — Harlan Estate, Opus One, and cult Cab programs are well-represented. Burgundy heads beyond the obvious Grand Crus into serious producer territory. The Rhône section shows they're not just chasing trophy bottles — there's actual curation happening here across all major regions.
reviews.byTheGlass: With 50-60 pours available, the by-the-glass program is more robust than most restaurants' entire lists. You'll find serious wines here, not just entry-level crowd pleasers. The rotation appears steady rather than adventurous, which makes sense for a steakhouse clientele that wants consistency over experimentation.

bestValue.wine: Unable to specify without concrete pricing data
bestValue.note: With this markup level and luxury positioning, 'value' is relative — focus on wine quality over bargain hunting here

hiddenGem.note: While everyone fights over the Bordeaux and Napa allocations, the Rhône offerings likely deliver serious quality at less eye-watering prices

skipThis.note: At steakhouse markup on a list this deep, the generic Napa Cabs are going to hurt — trade up or pivot to another region entirely

perfectPairing.note: Classic pairing for a reason — the structured tannins of aged Pauillac soften beautifully against butter-tender beef

reviews.bottomLine: If you're celebrating something big or entertaining a client with an expense account, this is where you go in Dallas. The markup stings, but the cellar depth and sommelier expertise justify the premium for serious wine occasions.
reviews.subtitle: Dallas's 3,500-bottle power move for serious collectors

reviews.firstImpression: You open the list and immediately realize you're in serious territory — Romanée-Conti, Screaming Eagle, Château Margaux sit alongside 1000+ other bottles. But then the by-the-glass section hits: 30-40 pours including Le Mesnil Blanc de Blancs at $25 and Graham's 20 Year Tawny at $19. This is how you do luxury without the attitude.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: The cellar goes absurdly deep with blue-chip Burgundy and Bordeaux, but the real story is the breadth beyond the trophy bottles. They're exploring rising French regions and Atlantic Spanish wines from Galicia and Castilla y León — not just checking boxes with Rioja and Priorat. The Penfolds Grange signals serious Australian representation. With over 1000 bottles, there's room for both the collectors chasing DRC and the curious exploring Pigato from Liguria. This isn't a greatest-hits compilation; it's a cellar built by someone who actually drinks wine.
reviews.byTheGlass: Thirty to forty glass pours at a fine dining hotel is almost unheard of, and they're not pouring house plonk. Le Mesnil Blanc de Blancs Champagne for $25 is stunning value, Vietti Moscato d'Asti at $13 shows range, and the Claudio Vio Pigato at $20 proves they're taking risks on lesser-known producers. The glass program alone could carry a neighborhood wine bar.

bestValue.wine: Le Mesnil Blanc de Blancs Grand Cru Champagne NV
bestValue.note: Grower Champagne at retail prices — this bottle goes for $60 in shops, and they're pouring it by the glass for less than half that

hiddenGem.wine: Claudio Vio Pigato Riviera Ligure di Ponente
hiddenGem.note: Ligurian white that nobody orders because they don't know it exists — minerally, herbal, electric with seafood, and priced at $20 glass when it should cost more

skipThis.note: At $25 glass it's the only wine on the list marked up above retail — everything else is shockingly fair, so why overpay for the dessert wine?

perfectPairing.note: Sounds wrong until you try it — the caramelized fruit in aged tawny echoes the soup's roasted peppers and the slight oxidation cuts through cream while the sweetness plays off smoke and spice

reviews.bottomLine: This is what happens when a luxury property actually respects wine drinkers instead of gouging them. A 1000-bottle cellar with fair pricing and a glass program that rivals dedicated wine bars — we'd send anyone here, from first-timers to collectors.
reviews.subtitle: Dallas's 1000-Bottle Palace With Shocking Glass Pours

reviews.firstImpression: This is a French bistro that actually commits to the bit. The wine list leans heavily into Provence and the Southern Rhône, with a respectful nod to Burgundy and a few international outliers. It's clear someone here cares about pairing wine with cassoulet, not just filling slots on a menu.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: The list runs 100-150 bottles with a strong Provençal rosé game and Southern Rhône depth that makes sense for the cuisine. You'll find solid Burgundy selections for those willing to spend, plus some international bottles to keep things from feeling too one-note. The focus is unapologetically French, which is exactly what you want in a place called Lavendou. No gimmicks, no Australian Shiraz for the sake of completion—just wines that belong on a French bistro table.
reviews.byTheGlass: With 15-20 pours available, the by-the-glass program is more than serviceable. The selections skew toward approachable French wines in the $30-60 bottle range, meaning glasses are priced to drink without wincing. They rotate seasonally but don't go wild with obscure picks—this is about reliable quality, not sommelier flexing.

bestValue.note: Classic food-friendly rosé at bistro pricing—perfect with moules frites and won't wreck your wallet

hiddenGem.wine: Southern Rhône red (Côtes du Rhône or similar)
hiddenGem.note: Everyone sleeps on these workhorses, but they're built for rich French bistro fare and priced like they want you to actually drink them

skipThis.note: If you're splurging on Burgundy, do it somewhere with a deeper cellar—these feel like obligatory list-fillers

perfectPairing.note: Provence's answer to mussels and fries—the wine's structure and salinity meet the briny shellfish head-on

reviews.bottomLine: Lavendou gets the assignment. The list is thoughtful, fairly priced, and actually matches what's on the plate. If you want to drink French wine with French food in North Dallas, this is your spot.
reviews.subtitle: Provençal Charm With a Wine List to Match

reviews.firstImpression: You're 49 floors up looking at a leather-bound tome with 300+ bottles, and the server actually knows what's in it. This is the kind of list that makes you consider ordering wine before you look at food—if you've got the credit limit for it.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: The list leans hard into Napa Valley with serious depth—we're talking verticals from top producers and library selections that most restaurants wouldn't touch. Schramsberg anchors the sparkling section with their Blanc de Blancs, while Cain Vineyards gets hand-selected representation that you won't find at your corner wine shop. The Italian section backs up the cuisine credibly, though Napa clearly gets top billing. At 300+ bottles, there's enough range to keep collectors and casual drinkers both engaged, even if the price tags skew toward expense-account territory.
reviews.byTheGlass: 25-30 pours by the glass is generous for a restaurant at this level, and they're rotating things with wine dinners featuring producers like Cain Vineyards. The selection runs the spectrum from approachable bubbles to serious Napa Cabs, so you're not stuck with house pours if you're not committing to a full bottle. Expect proper stemware and knowledgeable pours—they're taking the glass program seriously.

bestValue.note: Producer-direct sparkling that drinks way above its price point and pairs with everything from caviar service to those dry-aged steaks

hiddenGem.note: Most people default to the obvious Napa names, but Cain's Bordeaux-blend approach offers complexity you won't find in fruit-bomb Cabs at similar price points

skipThis.note: Solid producer but the markup at this altitude gets steep—you're paying for the view as much as the vineyard

perfectPairing.note: Classic pairing that works because the bubbles cut through the richness while the wine's elegance matches the luxury

reviews.bottomLine: If you've got the budget and you're serious about Napa Valley, Monarch delivers the depth and service to justify the splurge. Just know you're paying a premium for that 49th-floor cellar.
reviews.subtitle: Sky-High Steaks Meet Napa's Finest

reviews.firstImpression: The Standard Pour knows what it is: a neighborhood cocktail bar with exposed brick and bourbon vibes. The wine list reads like someone ordered the basics from a restaurant supply catalog and called it a day.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: You're looking at about 12 wines total—mostly entry-level crowd pleasers from supermarket brands like Villa Pozzi, Matua, and JP Chenet. There's a token nod to variety with picks from New Zealand, Italy, France, and Germany, but nothing shows any curatorial effort. No serious producers, no vintage depth, no local love. This is a list designed to exist, not to excite.
reviews.byTheGlass: Five by-the-glass options at $10-$12 cover the basics: Crowded House Sauvignon Blanc, Villa Pozzi Pinot Grigio, Macon Villages Chardonnay, Zeppelin Riesling, and a rosé. They're drinkable and inoffensive, but there's zero rotation and nothing you couldn't find at Target for less.

bestValue.note: Mosel Riesling by the glass for $11 isn't offensive—it's off-dry, food-friendly, and won't wreck your wallet before the bourbon pours start

hiddenGem.note: Still overpriced but it's actual Burgundy from a real producer, which makes it the most serious wine on the list by default

skipThis.note: They're charging $40 for a bottle that retails for $10—a 300% markup on bottom-shelf pink wine is insulting

perfectPairing.note: Bright bubbles cut through the sesame-soy flavors and avocado richness—plus at $11/glass it's the safest bet on the list

reviews.bottomLine: Come for the bourbon bacon chicken and waffle, stay for the Old Fashioneds, but order wine only if you've already had three cocktails and stopped caring about value. This is a cocktail bar that tolerates wine, not celebrates it.
reviews.subtitle: Cocktail Bar with an Afterthought Wine List

reviews.firstImpression: The wine list feels like it was built by someone who went to Tuscany once and never looked back. It's heavy on Italian crowd-pleasers — Chianti, Barolo, Amarone — with a few California standards to keep the steak crowd happy. Nothing adventurous, but nothing offensive either.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: With 50-75 bottles, Ravenna's list sticks to the classics: Tuscany dominates with Ruffino Chianti and Querceto Chianti Classico, while Piedmont brings Rocche Costamagna Barolo and Veneto offers Recchia Amarone. The American side leans predictable with Joseph Carr and Stags' Leap Cabernets from Napa, plus Four Graces Pinot from Willamette. You won't find any natural wines, orange wines, or boundary-pushing producers here. It's a list designed not to confuse anyone ordering the Filet Mignon.
reviews.byTheGlass: Eight glass pours ranging $9-$15 is workable but not inspiring. The selection appears to mirror the bottle list's safe approach — expect Chianti, a Napa Cab, maybe a Pinot. No evidence of rotation or seasonal changes, so you're likely getting the same pours in January as you are in July.

bestValue.note: Argentina fruit at a 200% markup sounds steep until you realize this retails for $15 — that's actually restaurant-fair pricing in Dallas, and it'll handle the Lobster Ravioli's richness

hiddenGem.note: Most people skip Barolo at an Italian-American spot, assuming it's overpriced or poorly stored, but Costamagna is a serious producer and if they're keeping it properly, it's the most interesting wine on the list

skipThis.note: Fine wine, but you can buy this at Target for $12 — order something you can't grab on grocery day

perfectPairing.note: Sangiovese's bright acidity cuts through the ricotta and red sauce without fighting the tomato — this is why Chianti exists

reviews.bottomLine: Ravenna plays it safe with fair markups and a Tuscan-heavy list that won't surprise anyone. It's a solid neighborhood choice when you want Italian wine with your Italian food, but don't expect the staff to guide you beyond the basics or the list to change much year to year.
reviews.subtitle: Tuscan-focused list with safe picks and fair pricing

reviews.firstImpression: Perry's hits you with a 300-bottle list that reads like a corporate buying guide done competently—heavy on California prestige names and safe Old World picks. The glass program is robust at 18+ pours, and prices start reasonable ($9) before climbing fast into trophy territory.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: The list spans California's greatest hits (Napa, Sonoma, Monterey), France's classic regions (Burgundy, Bordeaux, Rhône, Alsace), and Italian heavy-hitters from Tuscany to Piedmont, plus solid Northwest and New Zealand representation. Perry's house wines anchor the affordable end—their Reserve line covers Champagne, Chardonnay, Rosé, Pinot, and Cab from respectable producers. The top shelf flexes predictably: Opus One at $550, Caymus Special Selection at $400, Far Niente spanning $139-$265. It's a steakhouse playbook executed with more depth than most chains, but don't expect sommelier-curated surprises or cult producers.
reviews.byTheGlass: Eighteen-plus options by the glass is genuinely useful, ranging from $9 to $26. The spread covers Perry's Reserve line plus rotating selections that hit the usual suspects—California Cab, French Burgundy, Italian reds. It's corporate wine insurance: you won't find anything exciting, but you won't get burned either.

bestValue.note: Sonoma Cab at entry-level bottle pricing—solid fruit and structure without the Napa tax, perfect for their Prime steaks

hiddenGem.note: Monterey Rosé at a steakhouse? Sounds wrong but cuts beautifully through their Famous Pork Chop's richness—most people skip straight to red and miss it

skipThis.note: $550 for a bottle that retails around $350—this is trophy hunting markup at its worst, pure steakhouse flex pricing

perfectPairing.note: Buttery Napa Chard meets crispy fried asparagus with hollandaise—textbook California steakhouse decadence that actually works

reviews.bottomLine: Perry's delivers a safe, competent wine program that won't embarrass you at a client dinner but won't thrill the wine geeks either. Fair enough for a chain steakhouse—just watch those markup landmines on the prestige bottles.
reviews.subtitle: Corporate Done Right: Bottles That Match the Beef

reviews.firstImpression: Nearly 150 bottles at an upscale Mexican spot in Fort Worth — and they're leaning hard into wines from northern Mexico's Coahuila region alongside heavy-hitting Spanish and Californian producers. A sommelier on staff signals they're taking this seriously, but those markups tell a different story.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: This list swings for the fences with Mexican producers like Casa Madero and Tuma from Coahuila's wine country — a genuinely cool angle that most Mexican restaurants ignore entirely. They back it up with serious Spanish firepower from Vega Sicilia and Bodegas Ordóñez, plus California names like Aperture and Chimney Rock. The bubbles section alone runs from accessible Campo Viejo to proper Champagne like Taittinger and Moët & Chandon. It's ambitious and eclectic, but 150 selections means some bottles are sitting around collecting dust.
reviews.byTheGlass: The research doesn't reveal specific by-the-glass options, which is a red flag for a list this deep. With a sommelier on staff and this much inventory, we'd expect a rotating BTG program to showcase those Mexican producers and move wine. If it exists, they're not advertising it — and that's a missed opportunity.

bestValue.note: Northern Mexico's oldest winery delivers serious Cabernet that'll surprise doubters — and it's likely one of the more fairly priced bottles here given the markup pattern elsewhere

hiddenGem.note: Mexican Shiraz from Coahuila sounds like a gimmick until you try it — bold, sun-soaked fruit with actual structure, and nobody else in Dallas-Fort Worth is pouring it

skipThis.note: At $36 for a $12 retail bottle, this is a 200% markup on grocery store bubbles — there are better sparkling options on this list that justify the spend

perfectPairing.note: Mexican beef tongue meets Mexican Cabernet — the wine's structure and dark fruit cut through the richness while the regional connection makes it a no-brainer pairing

reviews.bottomLine: Don Artemio is doing something genuinely interesting with Mexican wine, but those steep markups (hello, $58 for a $20 bottle of El Bajío) keep it from being a full Rager. Still, for the adventurous drinker willing to pay the premium, this is the most exciting Mexican wine program in the Fort Worth area.
reviews.subtitle: 150 bottles deep with Mexican wine swagger

reviews.firstImpression: This is not your typical Dallas wine list. Chef Gorji's curated selection reads like a passport stamp collection — Lebanese reds, Georgian whites, Greek Nebbiolo — matched to his New Mediterranean cooking. The list is small but deliberate, with a sommelier-driven focus on lesser-known regions that actually make sense with the food.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: You'll find maybe 30-40 bottles total, but the range is genuinely exciting. There's Texas representation (Reddy Vineyard, Texas Hills Sangiovese from High Plains), classic French touchstones (Sancerre, Alsace), and then the wild cards: Ktima Karipidis Nebbiolo from Greece, Chateau Ksara from Lebanon, Spanish Ribera del Duero. The list tilts Old World and Mediterranean-adjacent, which tracks perfectly with pomegranate-glazed beef and stuffed eggplants. No bloated Napa Cab section here — this is a list built around the food, not ego.
reviews.byTheGlass: Ten to fifteen pours rotate through, priced $10-$15, which feels honest for a fine dining spot. The glass selection mirrors the bottle list's global bent — you're as likely to see Spanish Cava or Texas wine as California standards. The sommelier clearly adjusts the pour menu based on what's working, and with regular wine dinners, the by-the-glass options stay fresh.

bestValue.note: Traditional method sparkler from Catalonia that drinks like Champagne for half the price — perfect with Gorji's seafood-forward paella

hiddenGem.note: Greek Nebbiolo sounds like a gimmick until you taste it — earthy, structured, and a conversation starter that actually pairs with Persian-spiced lamb

skipThis.note: Likely marked up 3.5x+ retail and too heavy for most of the menu — save your money for something more list-appropriate

perfectPairing.dish: Prime beef tenderloin with pomegranate sauce
perfectPairing.note: Ribera del Duero's earthy Tempranillo has the structure for beef and the fruit-forward profile that bridges to pomegranate's sweet-tart punch

reviews.bottomLine: Gorji is the wine geek's secret weapon in Dallas — a small, thoughtfully chosen list that prioritizes Mediterranean and Middle Eastern bottles you won't find at the steakhouse next door. The sommelier knows their stuff, the pricing is fair, and the wine dinners prove this isn't just windowdressing. Send your adventurous friends here.
reviews.subtitle: Mediterranean Soul Meets Unexpected Global Pours

reviews.firstImpression: Isla & Co. brings a thoughtful, approachable wine program that mirrors their coastal-casual dining concept. The list jumps from Texas producers to classic French regions with enough by-the-glass options to keep things interesting without overwhelming.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: The list balances local pride with Old World credibility — you'll find Duchman representing Texas alongside proper Burgundy from Mat Roty. Their natural and organic wine focus shows intention, even if the execution leans safe. The Gascony white suggests someone on the team knows their way around lesser-known French regions. Glass pours run $12-$13, bottles $45-$80, which tracks fair for Dallas.
reviews.byTheGlass: Twenty to thirty glass pours is a solid spread for a restaurant of this style. The range hits the expected crowd-pleasers like Kim Crawford Sauvignon Blanc but also swings for Meursault at $13 a glass. No evidence of active rotation, but the breadth means you won't get bored on repeat visits.

bestValue.note: Standard bearer done right — crisp New Zealand SB at a fair price for both glass and bottle service

hiddenGem.note: Texas wine still gets underestimated nationally, and Duchman makes serious bottles worth exploring beyond the coastal fish dishes

skipThis.note: $80 bottle for Meursault isn't outrageous, but at $13/glass the markup stings when you could get more interesting white Burgundy elsewhere

perfectPairing.note: Gascony whites have that bright acidity and mineral edge to cut through fried batter while their body stands up to the richness

reviews.bottomLine: Isla & Co. delivers a wine program that won't blow your mind but won't disappoint either. Fair prices, smart regional picks, and enough variety to make coastal dining more interesting than the usual suspects.
reviews.subtitle: Coastal Vibes with Smart Texas-to-France Range

reviews.firstImpression: Walk into 55 Seventy and you'll immediately notice this isn't your typical Dallas steakhouse. This private members' club operates on a model so radical it sounds like a wine lover's fever dream: retail pricing on a 200-300 bottle list packed with allocated Burgundy, rare Napa cabs, and Austrian unicorns. The room feels like someone's sophisticated living room, because it basically is—membership required.
reviews.selectionDeepDive: The list leans hard into boutique and allocated bottles you won't find at your neighborhood Total Wine. Burgundies sourced through Peter Wasserman's network sit alongside serious Napa Valley reds and a surprisingly deep Austrian selection that shows someone on staff actually cares about Grüner and Blaufränkisch. Billecart-Salmon anchors the Champagne section with proper vintage bottles, not just the entry-level brut. The 20-30 glass pours rotate thoughtfully, giving you a real taste of the cellar without committing to a full bottle. This is a list built for wine geeks who know what they're looking for, not casual drinkers hoping for a safe Caymus.
reviews.byTheGlass: Twenty to thirty rotating glass pours is ambitious for any restaurant, let alone one doing retail pricing. The selection mirrors the bottle list's philosophy—serious, thoughtful, and focused on producers with a point of view. Expect Austrian whites, boutique Burgundy villages, and the occasional allocated Napa pour that would cost you $45 anywhere else but rings in at actual retail here.

bestValue.note: Any Burgundy at retail pricing is borderline theft, but Wasserman's sourcing means you're getting producer-direct quality without the usual 3x-4x restaurant markup

hiddenGem.note: Most Dallas diners sleep on Austria, but 55 Seventy's selection shows real depth—these are food-friendly, age-worthy bottles that pair brilliantly with their steaks and won't drain your wallet

skipThis.note: With access to allocated and rare bottles at retail, why waste the trip on safe, widely available wines you could buy at any shop?

perfectPairing.note: The acidity and minerality in Billecart cuts through the richness of prime steaks like a knife, and the vintage expression adds enough weight to stand up to serious meat

reviews.bottomLine: If you can score a membership, 55 Seventy is the best wine deal in Dallas, period. Retail pricing on allocated bottles in a setting where the staff actually knows what they're pouring? That's not just rare—it's practically unheard of.
reviews.subtitle: Members-Only Wine Paradise at Retail Prices
